Many Sierra Leonean farmers treat farming primarily as a way of life rather than as a deliberate business, failing to systematically track costs, yields, and sales, which means they often genuinely do not know whether a given season was actually profitable, and consequently cannot learn in any structured way from their own past mistakes or successes. A farmer may continue raising broilers for several consecutive cycles convinced the enterprise is profitable, purely on the basis of cash received at sale, without ever subtracting the cumulative cost of feed, chicks, and labour to discover the true picture.
3. Limited access to quality seeds and planting materials. Farmers frequently replant saved, often genetically degenerated seed of unknown quality rather than investing in certified improved varieties, resulting in persistently low and unpredictable yields regardless of how much genuine labour and care is otherwise invested in the crop. The same applies to livestock genetics, where a farmer repeatedly breeding from whatever local animals happen to be available, without any deliberate selection for productivity, can see herd or flock performance gradually decline over successive generations rather than improve.
4. Inadequate access to fertiliser and other essential agro-inputs. High retail prices, late seasonal delivery, and generally limited rural distribution networks mean that many farmers apply far less fertiliser and crop protection than their soils and crops actually require, capping realistic yields well below the genuine potential of the land they are farming. A farmer who can only afford to fertilise a fraction of the recommended amount each season effectively pays for fertiliser without ever capturing the full yield response that proper application would have delivered.
5. Heavy dependence on rainfall with little or no supplementary irrigation. With minimal irrigation infrastructure available to most farmers, a single poorly distributed rainy season, whether through a late start, an early end, or a damaging mid-season dry spell, can devastate an entire year's harvest regardless of how skilfully the farmer otherwise managed the crop throughout the season. Because most households depend on a single annual cropping cycle tied to the rains, a single bad season can mean an entire year without adequate income, with no second planting opportunity available to recover the loss.
6. High and steadily rising cost of farm labour. As more rural youth migrate to urban areas or abroad in search of better opportunities, hired farm labour has become noticeably scarcer and more expensive, squeezing farmers who cannot afford machinery as a practical alternative and forcing many to leave significant portions of their own land uncultivated each season. An older farmer whose own children have left for the city may simply be physically unable to cultivate the full extent of land they legally hold, regardless of how much they might wish to expand production.
7. Severe post-harvest losses due to poor storage and processing facilities. Without adequate drying floors, proper storage barns, or even basic processing equipment, a large share of harvested produce, especially perishable crops and stored grains during the rainy season, rots, suffers pest damage, or otherwise loses commercial quality before it can ever reach the market, substantially eroding the real value of the farmer's preceding effort. It is not uncommon for a farmer to lose a meaningful share of an entire rice harvest to weevils and moisture damage within just a few weeks of storage in a poorly ventilated traditional barn.
8. Weak market access and persistently poor price information. Many farming communities remain physically far from major markets, lack reliable transport options, and have no practical access to real-time price information, leaving farmers heavily dependent on local middlemen who often pay correspondingly low farm-gate prices, far below what the very same produce ultimately fetches in urban markets just a short distance away. A farmer selling vegetables at the farm gate may receive only a small fraction of the price the same produce later commands in a Freetown market, with the difference absorbed entirely by intermediaries the farmer has no practical way to bypass.
9. Price volatility and the lack of any guaranteed market outlet. Without contract farming arrangements or reliably stable buyers in place, farmers who do manage to produce a genuine surplus often find that prices crash sharply at harvest time precisely when everyone in the area is trying to sell simultaneously, undermining much of the income gain they had reasonably expected from an otherwise good harvest. A farmer who invested heavily to produce an unusually large tomato harvest may, paradoxically, end up earning less overall than in a more modest season, simply because the local market cannot absorb the additional volume without prices collapsing.
10. Land tenure insecurity and persistent land disputes. Much farmland across the country is held under customary tenure systems where boundaries remain genuinely unclear and disputes between extended families, neighbouring chiefdoms, or with returning war-displaced persons are common, discouraging long-term investment in land improvement and, in some unfortunate cases, ultimately resulting in farmers losing access altogether to land they had cultivated faithfully for many years. A farmer who has spent years establishing a tree crop plantation may find the underlying land suddenly contested by a returning relative or neighbouring family, placing years of investment and labour at genuine risk.
11. Limited and chronically underfunded agricultural extension services. With far too few trained extension officers covering very large rural areas, many farmers receive little or no genuinely professional advice on improved practices, emerging pest and disease threats, or changing market opportunities, leaving them to rely largely on outdated traditional methods even in cases where demonstrably better alternatives already exist and are simply not reaching them. A single extension officer responsible for dozens of widely scattered villages can realistically visit each community only rarely, leaving most farmers to rely on word of mouth and personal trial and error for most of their technical knowledge.
12. Pest and disease outbreaks. Crop pests such as the fall armyworm and livestock diseases such as Newcastle disease or African swine fever can destroy a farmer's entire seasonal investment within a matter of days or weeks, especially where early warning systems and access to appropriate, genuinely affordable treatments remain weak or altogether absent. A poultry keeper who has built up a flock over many months can lose the entire flock within a single week to an unvaccinated Newcastle disease outbreak, erasing what may have been the household's single largest economic asset almost overnight.
13. Climate change and increasingly unpredictable weather patterns. Shifting rainfall patterns, more intense and damaging storms, and gradually rising temperatures are steadily making the traditional farming calendar less reliable than it once was, repeatedly catching farmers off guard when planting decisions are based on long-established patterns that no longer hold as consistently as they once did. A farmer who plants according to the timing their parents and grandparents always used may now find the rains arriving weeks later, or stopping weeks earlier, than the patterns that guided previous generations.
14. Poor rural infrastructure, particularly impassable roads. Roads that become genuinely impassable during the rainy season, which unfortunately coincides with the harvest period for many important crops, can prevent farmers from getting their produce to market at all, forcing distress sales right at the farm gate or, in the worst cases, outright total loss of the harvest. A farmer in a remote community may watch produce rot in storage simply because no vehicle can reach the village until the road dries out, by which point the harvest has already lost most of its commercial value.
15. Limited mechanization, forcing continued heavy reliance on manual labour. As detailed extensively above, the persistently slow pace of mechanization keeps most farm operations stubbornly labour-intensive and time-consuming, sharply limiting the realistic area any individual household can cultivate and, in turn, capping their potential income regardless of personal ambition or work ethic. A highly motivated farming household, working entirely by hand, may simply reach a hard physical ceiling on how much land it can prepare and plant within the available season, regardless of how much additional suitable land might otherwise be available to them.
16. Low levels of formal education and technical agricultural training among farmers. Many farmers, having had only limited access to schooling themselves, genuinely struggle to access, properly understand, or correctly apply new technical information, written extension materials, or basic financial literacy concepts that are increasingly needed to professionalise and meaningfully grow their farming enterprises beyond mere subsistence. A farmer unable to read a feed label or calculate a simple dosage rate may unintentionally misuse an input, wasting money or even harming their crop or livestock through incorrect application.
17. Gender inequality in access to land, credit, and household decision-making. Women, who collectively carry out a very large share of actual day-to-day farm labour across Sierra Leone, frequently lack equal rights to own land outright, to access credit in their own name, or to independently control the proceeds of sale, limiting both their individual economic prospects and overall household agricultural productivity as a direct result. A woman who manages the family's vegetable garden or small livestock day to day may nonetheless have little real say over whether income from selling produce is reinvested in the farm or spent on other household priorities.
18. Insufficient government investment and persistent policy inconsistency. Agricultural budgets are often comparatively small relative to the sector's genuine economic importance to the country, and government priorities and supporting programmes can shift frequently with changes in administration or the natural cycles of donor funding, undermining the long-term, sustained support that genuine agricultural transformation actually requires to take root. A farmer who has come to rely on a particular subsidy or extension programme may find it discontinued with little warning once political or funding priorities shift elsewhere.
19. Youth disinterest in farming and continued rural-urban migration. Many young Sierra Leoneans understandably view farming as unattractive, physically demanding, comparatively low-status, and insufficiently profitable when set against urban informal trade or migration abroad, steadily draining rural areas of the energetic labour and fresh innovative potential genuinely needed to modernise the sector from within. An ageing farmer with no children remaining in the village to eventually take over the farm may simply allow productive land to fall fallow once they are no longer physically able to work it themselves.
20. Limited access to agricultural insurance and other formal risk management tools. With virtually no crop or livestock insurance products realistically available to ordinary smallholders, a single genuinely bad season, disease outbreak, flood, or fire can wipe out a farmer's entire accumulated capital in one stroke, and can permanently discourage them, or their immediate family, from ever returning to farming as a serious livelihood again afterward. A farmer who loses an entire herd to disease, with no insurance payout or compensation scheme to fall back on, may be forced to abandon livestock keeping entirely rather than attempt the difficult task of rebuilding from nothing.

💡 Recommended

  • Treat this list as a pre-investment checklist — before committing capital, ask which of these twenty risks apply to your specific situation.
  • Where formal insurance isn't available, diversify across several smaller enterprises (see section 8.0) rather than relying on a single crop or animal.

15.0 CONCLUSION

🔑 Key Points

  • Animal husbandry, beekeeping, aquaculture, and under-exploited agro-enterprises together offer a clear path to better nutrition, rural jobs, and reduced import dependence.
  • Realising this potential requires honestly addressing financing, infrastructure, skills, land tenure, and market-access barriers.
  • Top priorities: affordable credit and record-keeping skills, sustained (not just donor-cycle) investment in infrastructure and extension, diversification into under-exploited enterprises, and deliberate inclusion of women and youth.

Animal husbandry, alongside its closely allied enterprises of beekeeping and aquaculture, and the wider universe of under-exploited agro-enterprises such as snail farming, grasscutter rearing, mushroom cultivation, and vermiculture, represents one of the clearest available pathways for Sierra Leone to improve household nutrition, generate genuine rural and youth employment, and meaningfully reduce the country's continuing dependence on imported animal protein. Institutions such as Njala University, with their century-long heritage in agricultural research and education, are absolutely central to supplying the trained extension officers, researchers, and entrepreneurs that this kind of transformation genuinely requires, and the university's explicit, deliberate alignment with national priorities such as the Feed Salone agenda underscores the strategic role higher education must continue to play in driving forward agricultural development at the national level.

Yet realising this very real potential demands an honest, unflinching confrontation of the structural and practical obstacles that have long held the sector back, from the financing, infrastructure, and skills challenges that continue to constrain mechanization, to the deeper, often interlocking reasons, ranging from basic capital constraints and persistent land tenure insecurity to mounting climate variability and weak market access, that cause so many individual farming ventures across Sierra Leone to consistently underperform or, in far too many cases, to fail outright despite the genuine effort invested. Addressing these challenges in any lasting way will require carefully coordinated action across government policy, research and extension institutions, private sector investment, and farmer-level capacity building alike, but the underlying natural resource base and human potential needed to support a far more productive, resilient, and genuinely diversified Sierra Leonean agricultural sector is, by any honest and realistic assessment, already there, waiting to be more fully and deliberately developed.

If a small number of priorities had to be singled out from everything covered in this report, they would be these: deliberately strengthening farmer access to affordable credit and basic record-keeping skills, so that good decisions can actually be acted upon once they are made; investing consistently, and beyond the lifespan of any single donor project, in rural infrastructure, extension services, and appropriately scaled mechanization; actively diversifying household income through the many under-exploited enterprises described in this report, from beekeeping and aquaculture to snail and grasscutter farming, rather than relying on a single crop or animal alone; and ensuring that women, youth, and other often-overlooked groups are deliberately included, rather than incidentally assumed to benefit, in every one of these efforts. None of these priorities is beyond Sierra Leone's reach, and institutions such as Njala University, alongside government, development partners, and the farmers themselves, already possess much of the knowledge, experience, and goodwill needed to pursue them, provided the sustained commitment to do so can finally be matched to the scale of the opportunity at hand.

💡 Recommended

  • Revisit the Key Points boxes across sections 4-13 above as a quick-reference checklist before starting or expanding any livestock enterprise.
  • Consider combining at least one conventional livestock activity with one under-exploited enterprise from section 8.0 to spread risk.

16.0 TWENTY COMMON FARMING MYTHS IN SIERRA LEONE, DEBUNKED

Farming decisions across Sierra Leone are often shaped as much by long-held beliefs as by agronomic evidence. Many of these beliefs contain a grain of truth but are applied too broadly, while others are simply outdated. The myths below are common across smallholder communities, paired with the more accurate, evidence-based picture.

Bush burning is the best way to clear and fertilise land.
TRUTH

Burning destroys organic matter, soil microbes, and seed banks, and releases nutrients as smoke instead of returning them to the soil. Mulching, composting, and controlled slashing build soil fertility far more durably.

More fertiliser always means a bigger harvest.
TRUTH

Beyond a crop's actual nutrient need, excess fertiliser is wasted money, can burn roots, and leaches into waterways. Matching fertiliser type and quantity to soil test results and crop stage gives a better return than simply applying more.

Local/traditional seed varieties are always better than improved varieties.
TRUTH

Traditional varieties often have genuine advantages in local adaptation and taste, but many improved varieties bred for Sierra Leone's conditions out-yield them while keeping similar resilience. The better choice depends on the specific variety, not a blanket rule.

Pesticides should be applied on a fixed weekly schedule regardless of pest levels.
TRUTH

Spraying on a calendar instead of based on actual pest scouting wastes money, kills beneficial insects, and accelerates pest resistance. Integrated pest management based on monitoring is more effective and cheaper over a season.

Intercropping reduces yield because crops compete with each other.
TRUTH

Well-chosen intercrop combinations (such as cassava with groundnuts, or maize with cowpea) often produce more total food per hectare than the same land split into single-crop plots, while also spreading risk across more than one crop.

Bigger animals or plants always mean a more profitable farm.
TRUTH

Profit depends on cost per unit produced, not the size of the animal or plant. A smaller, well-managed enterprise with low losses often out-earns a larger one with poor record keeping and high mortality or wastage.

Organic farming with no inputs at all will always be more profitable than using any inputs.
TRUTH

Some inputs (improved seed, mineral fertiliser, vaccines) raise output enough to more than cover their cost. The real comparison should be cost versus benefit for each specific input, not an automatic preference for using none.

If a crop or animal looks healthy, it does not need vaccination or preventive treatment.
TRUTH

Many serious diseases (Newcastle disease, PPR, fall armyworm) spread before visible symptoms appear. Preventive vaccination and scouting catch problems before they become visible, and far less costly, losses.

Farming is only for people who failed at school or have no other option.
TRUTH

Modern agribusiness spans agronomy, veterinary science, mechanics, food processing, logistics, and finance — all needing real skill and training. Some of the most profitable agribusinesses in Sierra Leone are run by university-trained entrepreneurs.

Women cannot manage livestock or large farms effectively.
TRUTH

Women already perform a large share of livestock and crop labour across Sierra Leone, and women-led farms and cooperatives are not less productive when given equal access to land, credit, and training.

Rainfed farming does not need any irrigation planning.
TRUTH

Even rainfed systems benefit from supplementary irrigation or water harvesting during dry spells within the rainy season, which are becoming more frequent and unpredictable with climate change.

All chemical fertilisers are dangerous to human health.
TRUTH

Mineral fertilisers used at recommended rates and with basic protective measures are not directly hazardous to consumers; the real risks come from improper handling, overuse, or unsafe storage, not from fertiliser use itself.

A farm only succeeds if it owns all its own land and equipment.
TRUTH

Land leasing, sharecropping, and equipment-hire schemes let many successful farmers scale production without the upfront capital needed for outright ownership.

Crop rotation is unnecessary if you keep adding fertiliser.
TRUTH

Fertiliser replaces some nutrients but does not fix soil-borne pest and disease build-up, which rotation specifically interrupts. Continuous mono-cropping raises pest pressure over time regardless of fertiliser use.

Once a borehole or well is dug, water supply for livestock or irrigation is guaranteed forever.
TRUTH

Water tables fluctuate seasonally and with rainfall trends; boreholes and wells need monitoring, maintenance, and sometimes supplementary water-harvesting structures to remain reliable year-round.

Selling immediately after harvest is always the best strategy.
TRUTH

Prices are typically lowest right after harvest when supply is highest. Basic storage, drying, or simple processing to sell later, or to a more reliable buyer, often earns significantly more for the same produce.

Livestock manure is just waste that needs to be disposed of.
TRUTH

Manure is a valuable input for composting, biogas, and soil fertility when managed properly, and can itself become a saleable product rather than a disposal problem.

Farmers don't need formal record keeping if they "know their business."
TRUTH

Many otherwise successful-looking farms are quietly loss-making once true costs are tracked. Simple records consistently reveal where money is actually being made or lost, information memory alone cannot reliably hold.

Imported food is always of better quality than locally produced food.
TRUTH

Freshness, traceability, and nutrient retention often favour properly handled local produce. Import preference is frequently driven by perception and marketing rather than an actual quality difference.

Climate change is a future problem, not something affecting farming now.
TRUTH

Shifting rainfall onset, more erratic dry spells, and new pest pressures (such as fall armyworm's spread) are already measurably affecting Sierra Leonean farms today, not at some distant future date.

Compiled report · Animal Husbandry and Allied Agro-Enterprises in Sierra Leone.

From Hand Tools to Tractors

Tools, Equipment & Machines Reference

Over 100 hand tools, implements, and machines used across Sierra Leone's farms and workshops, organized by stage of work. Search or filter by category to find what a specific job needs.

🔎
Voice-Accessible & Offline

Interactive Vocabulary Glossary

Search any agronomic or technical term across 10 categories, or look up a common abbreviation. Every entry has a practical Farmer Definition next to a Student/Teacher Definition aligned with the Njala University and Sierra Leone National Curriculum.

🔎
🔎
Government & International Interventions

Government & Global Partners

The national strategy and international organizations actively investing in Sierra Leone's agricultural and mechanization transformation.

🇸🇱 Feed Salone — Sierra Leone's Flagship Agricultural Strategy

Feed Salone is President Julius Maada Bio's flagship agricultural transformation strategy, drawn from the Sierra Leone People's Party 2023 manifesto, aimed at boosting productivity, cutting food import dependence, raising export earnings, creating jobs, and building a climate-resilient food system by 2030. It is led by the Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security under Minister Henry Musa Kpaka, with support from FAO, the EU, IFAD, WFP, and UNCDF among others. Njala University was one of the named technical partners that helped formulate the strategy alongside SLARI, IITA, and AfricaRice — putting this Department's own work directly inside the national effort it teaches about. The strategy includes a $75 million Special Agro-Processing Zone project and a graduated rice import duty designed to encourage local production over imports, delivered through six strategic pillars detailed below.

Pillar 1 — Seed & Input Systems

This pillar focuses on getting improved, locally adapted seed varieties, such as the NERICA and ROK-5 rice lines referenced throughout this portal's glossary and resources, into farmers' hands reliably each season, alongside the fertilizer and other inputs needed to grow them well. SLARI's variety development work and a more organized national seed multiplication and distribution chain sit at the centre of this pillar, since improved varieties only raise national yields if the seed actually reaches smallholders before planting season, which has historically been an inconsistent and under-resourced part of the system.

Pillar 2 — Mechanization & Irrigation

This is the direct national policy home of everything taught on this portal. The pillar covers expanding tractor and equipment access through Agricultural Business Centres and private hire schemes, building and rehabilitating irrigation infrastructure for inland valley swamps and bolilands, and addressing the training and maintenance gaps that the Sierra Leone Audit Service identified as the cause of early equipment failure in earlier mechanization schemes. Strengthening this pillar is explicitly meant to close Sierra Leone's mechanization density gap with the rest of West Africa and the wider world.

Pillar 3 — Aggregation, Processing & Marketing

This pillar moves harvested crops from scattered individual farms into organized markets and processing facilities, anchored by the $75 million Special Agro-Processing Zone project and supported by FAO's technical assistance in developing the implementation plan. A graduated import duty on rice, rising over several years, is designed to make locally aggregated and processed rice more price-competitive against imports, directly targeting the roughly 480,000 tonnes of rice Sierra Leone was importing annually before the strategy began.

Pillar 4 — Agricultural Finance

This pillar tackles the rural credit gap that leaves most smallholders unable to finance seed, fertilizer, or equipment access without it. The EU and UNCDF-backed Salone Access to Finance project, targeting roughly 500,000 farmers with credit, savings, and insurance products, and IFAD's Rural Finance and Community Improvement Programme both sit under this pillar, working to make the Equipment Loan Planner-style financing decisions modeled elsewhere on this portal genuinely accessible to ordinary farming households, not just larger commercial operations.

Pillar 5 — AgTech & Climate-Smart Agriculture

This pillar promotes digital and climate-resilient farming tools, from offline weather advisory apps like the Crop Calendar resource on this portal to broader climate-smart practices that help farming withstand Sierra Leone's shifting and increasingly compressed rainfall patterns. It reflects the same global pressure described in this portal's Why Mechanization section: climate change is narrowing the reliable planting window everywhere, and digital tools that help farmers time land preparation and planting more precisely are becoming as important as the machinery itself.

Pillar 6 — Empowering Women & Youth

This cross-cutting pillar ensures the other five pillars actively include women and young people rather than defaulting to whoever already has land, capital, or equipment access, which in practice has often meant older male farmers. It supports youth agribusiness and equipment-hire ventures, and digital literacy with real tools like Esoko and Hello Tractor profiled in this portal's Resource Hub, and aims to make sure finance, training, and input programmes under the other pillars are genuinely reaching women farmers, who make up a large share of Sierra Leone's agricultural labor force.

International & National Partners

🌍
UN Agency

FAO — Food and Agriculture Organization

Founded in 1945 as one of the United Nations' first specialized agencies, FAO leads global efforts to defeat hunger and raise nutrition, agricultural productivity, and rural living standards across its 194 member nations. In Sierra Leone, FAO has worked directly with the Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security on developing the implementation plan for Feed Salone's aggregation, processing, and marketing pillar, and is a core partner behind the Africa Phytosanitary Programme that has equipped the Ministry's Crop Protection Unit with field diagnostic tools. FAO also maintains the global agricultural statistics and standards, including the tractor-density and yield figures referenced throughout this portal's Why Mechanization section, that let one country's agricultural performance be meaningfully compared with another's.

🌾
UN Fund

IFAD — International Fund for Agricultural Development

Established in 1977 specifically to address rural poverty and hunger as a dedicated UN financial institution, IFAD lends and grants money directly into rural agricultural development projects rather than general budget support. In Sierra Leone, IFAD co-finances the Rural Finance and Community Improvement Programme, which has connected hundreds of thousands of farming households to credit, savings groups, and insurance products many had never had access to before, and has separately partnered with the OPEC Fund for International Development on an Agricultural Value Chain Development Project targeting smallholder market access. IFAD's funding model, low-interest loans blended with grants, is specifically structured to reach poorer member countries that conventional commercial lending would otherwise overlook.

🍚
UN Agency

WFP — World Food Programme

The world's largest humanitarian organization addressing hunger, WFP both responds to emergencies and runs longer-term resilience and nutrition programmes in dozens of countries including Sierra Leone. Its Home-Grown School Feeding Programme is the clearest example of WFP's dual purpose locally: it purchases rice and pulses directly from registered Sierra Leonean smallholder cooperatives to supply school meals, simultaneously improving child nutrition and guaranteeing smallholders a stable buyer at a fair price, directly reinforcing the income goals of the Feed Salone strategy. WFP's local procurement model is part of a broader global shift in food aid away from importing donor-country surplus grain and toward buying from the same farmers the programme is trying to support.

🏦
Multilateral Development Bank

World Bank

Founded in 1944, the World Bank is one of the world's largest sources of development financing and technical assistance, lending to national governments for projects spanning infrastructure, finance, and agriculture. In Sierra Leone, the World Bank has funded large multi-year agricultural programmes including the Smallholder Commercialization and Agribusiness Development Project, which has directly built the institutional capacity of bodies like the Sierra Leone Chamber for Agribusiness Development and the National Federation of Farmers of Sierra Leone through staff training, office support, and equipment. The Bank typically works through the national government as the borrower and implementing agency, which is why so much of its visible impact shows up as capacity built inside Sierra Leone's own institutions rather than as a separately branded World Bank programme.

🌉
Regional Development Bank

AfDB — African Development Bank

Africa's own premier regional development finance institution, founded in 1964 and owned by its African member states alongside non-regional shareholders, AfDB finances infrastructure and agricultural development specifically tailored to African economic conditions. In Sierra Leone, AfDB previously funded the Agricultural Sector Rehabilitation Project, targeting post-civil-war recovery of rice production and farmer incomes, and has more recently co-funded, alongside the Gates Foundation, programmes such as the Technologies for African Agricultural Transformation initiative implemented through IITA. AfDB's High 5s strategic priorities explicitly include 'Feed Africa', making agricultural lending a structurally central part of the bank's mission rather than a peripheral activity.

⭐
Regional Bloc & Donor

European Union

As a bloc of 27 member states, the EU is one of the largest collective donors to agricultural development worldwide, channeling funding both bilaterally and through multilateral partners like FAO and UNCDF. In Sierra Leone, the EU co-financed the Salone Access to Finance project alongside UNCDF, specifically targeting financial inclusion for smallholder farmers, and separately backs FAO's technical work helping the Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security operationalize the Feed Salone strategy on the ground. EU agricultural development funding in West Africa is increasingly channelled through joint financing arrangements with other donors rather than stand-alone EU-branded projects, reflecting a broader donor-coordination push across the region.

💳
UN Agency

UNCDF — UN Capital Development Fund

A specialized UN agency focused specifically on expanding access to finance for the world's least developed countries, UNCDF works on digital financial inclusion, de-risking private investment, and building the financial infrastructure poorer economies often lack. Its flagship Sierra Leone initiative, the Salone Access to Finance project, co-launched with the EU and the national government, specifically targets roughly 500,000 farmers with credit, insurance, and digital financial services, directly addressing the rural finance gap that AI tools like Apollo Agriculture and ACRE Africa's TARA, both profiled elsewhere on this portal, are also built to close from the private-sector side. UNCDF's approach is distinctive in treating financial infrastructure itself, not just direct farmer payments, as the lasting development investment.

🇩🇪
Bilateral Agency (Germany)

GIZ — German Development Cooperation

Germany's principal implementing agency for international development cooperation, GIZ provides technical assistance, training, and institutional advisory support rather than large capital loans, making it complementary to bank-style financiers like AfDB or the World Bank. In Sierra Leone, GIZ has delivered vocational training and business coaching for small agricultural enterprises and has advised the Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security directly on institutional and technical capacity, the same kind of capacity-building emphasis that runs through this portal's own operator and mechanic certification syllabi. GIZ's German government funding model gives it more flexibility to support softer institutional capacity-building work than financiers whose mandate is primarily large infrastructure lending.

🇯🇵
Bilateral Agency (Japan)

JICA — Japan International Cooperation Agency

Japan's government development agency, JICA has a long-standing specialization in rice productivity that traces directly back to Japan's own historical experience intensifying rice yields after the Second World War. JICA is a founding partner, alongside AGRA and FAO, of the Coalition for African Rice Development, the same regional initiative that helped drive the development and spread of NERICA varieties referenced throughout this portal's glossary and resources, and South Korea's parallel agricultural cooperation produced the ROK rice variety series also referenced extensively here. JICA's technical cooperation model typically pairs Japanese agricultural specialists directly with national research institutes like SLARI, transferring specific crop-science expertise rather than only providing finance.

🇺🇸
Bilateral Agency (USA)

USAID

The United States' principal foreign assistance agency, USAID has historically been one of the largest bilateral funders of agricultural value chain development across West Africa, though the scale and structure of US foreign assistance has been subject to significant policy change in recent years, so current programme details should be confirmed against USAID's own current public reporting rather than assumed unchanged. In Sierra Leone, USAID-supported work has focused on rice and aquaculture value chains, farmer access to microfinance, and business training for producer associations and small agribusinesses. USAID's funding has typically flowed through implementing partner organizations and local producer associations rather than direct-to-farmer payments, building local institutional capacity to eventually operate independently of donor support.

🌾
CGIAR Research Centre

AfricaRice — Africa Rice Center

A specialized intergovernmental rice research centre and a core member of the CGIAR global research partnership, AfricaRice exists specifically because rice in Africa faces distinct agronomic and varietal challenges from rice in Asia, where most global rice science historically concentrated. AfricaRice developed the NERICA family of varieties referenced throughout this portal, bred specifically by crossing African and Asian rice species to combine African rice's resilience with Asian rice's yield potential, and the centre was a named technical partner, alongside Njala University, IITA, and SLARI, in formulating Sierra Leone's Feed Salone national strategy. AfricaRice's mangrove rice research specifically benefits Sierra Leone's relatively unusual deepwater and mangrove rice ecologies, a specialization few other rice research bodies worldwide maintain.

🧬
CGIAR Research Centre

IITA — International Institute of Tropical Agriculture

Founded in Nigeria in 1967, IITA is the lead CGIAR research centre for tropical staple crops including cassava, cowpea, and maize, and its decades of cassava breeding work underlie the improved varieties referenced throughout this portal's cassava resources. IITA developed and maintains AKILIMO, the AI-backed cassava decision-support tool profiled elsewhere on this portal, and is a direct technical partner to Sierra Leone's Feed Salone strategy, with IITA's Director General and SLARI leadership jointly convening national seed-system planning summits. IITA's BASICS project, funded by the Gates Foundation, and the AfDB and Gates Foundation co-funded Technologies for African Agricultural Transformation initiative both run cassava and seed-system work with direct Sierra Leone components.

🔬
Global Research Partnership

CGIAR

CGIAR is not a single organization but a worldwide partnership of agricultural research centres, including AfricaRice and IITA among more than a dozen others, coordinating publicly funded crop science aimed at smallholder farmers in developing countries rather than commercially patented agricultural technology. Much of the crop science underlying Sierra Leone's improved seed varieties, from NERICA rice to improved cassava lines, traces back through this CGIAR network rather than from any single national programme working in isolation. CGIAR's research is deliberately published openly and made available royalty-free to national breeding programmes like SLARI, which is part of why CGIAR-derived varieties spread so widely across different countries' agricultural systems.

🌱
Pan-African Organization

AGRA — Alliance for a Green Revolution in Africa

Founded in 2006 with the Rockefeller Foundation and the Gates Foundation among its founding backers, and with Kofi Annan as its first board chair, AGRA was built specifically to extend the productivity gains of Asia's mid-twentieth-century Green Revolution to African smallholder agriculture through improved seed systems, soil health programmes, and market access support. AGRA is a co-founding partner, alongside JICA and FAO, of the Coalition for African Rice Development launched in 2008, the same coalition whose work fed directly into the NERICA and ROK variety adoption referenced throughout this portal. AGRA's approach has also drawn sustained criticism from researchers and African civil society groups who argue its emphasis on commercial seed and fertilizer systems can sideline farmer seed-saving rights and local seed diversity, a genuine and ongoing debate worth knowing about rather than treating AGRA's model as uncontested.

🤝
Regional Bloc

ECOWAS & ECOWAP

The Economic Community of West African States, a 15-member regional bloc founded in 1975, runs a dedicated regional agricultural policy framework called ECOWAP aimed at harmonizing agricultural standards and reducing trade barriers between member states. For Sierra Leone specifically, ECOWAP-aligned regional research coordination through CORAF and WECARD has supported technology transfer in rice production between Sierra Leone and neighbouring WAATP member countries, including Sierra Leone's specialization in mangrove rice research feeding into a wider Regional Centre of Excellence network. ECOWAS's broader economic integration mandate, free movement of people, goods, and services across member states, also directly affects how easily mechanization equipment, spare parts, and even trained operators and mechanics can move between Sierra Leone and its neighbours.

🏛️
National Research Institute

SLARI — Sierra Leone Agricultural Research Institute

Sierra Leone's own national agricultural research institute, SLARI coordinates crop and livestock research across a network of specialized centres including the Rokupr Agricultural Research Centre for rice and the Njala Agricultural Research Centre, and was a named technical partner, alongside Njala University, IITA, and AfricaRice, in formulating the Feed Salone national strategy. SLARI's Director General has personally convened national seed-system summits bringing together IITA, AGRA, and major donors including the Gates Foundation and AfDB specifically to plan Sierra Leone's seed sector investment roadmap. As the institute responsible for adapting internationally developed varieties and practices to Sierra Leone's specific soils, climate, and farming systems, SLARI is the critical translation layer between global agricultural research and what actually works on a Sierra Leonean farm.

🐛
International Standard-Setting Body

IPPC & Africa Phytosanitary Programme

The International Plant Protection Convention is a global treaty framework, hosted by FAO, that sets the international standards for plant health and pest control that countries use to protect their own agriculture while still enabling safe agricultural trade. Sierra Leone is a pilot country for the IPPC-linked Africa Phytosanitary Programme, which has provided the Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security's Crop Protection Unit with training, pest-detection tools, and field tablets for digital pest reporting, infrastructure that gives the kind of early pest-outbreak warning system this portal's AI tools like Plantix and PlantVillage Nuru work alongside rather than replace. Phytosanitary standards also directly determine which Sierra Leonean agricultural exports, including cocoa, can legally enter certain international markets at all.

🎓
Foundation

Gates Foundation

One of the world's largest private philanthropic foundations, the Gates Foundation has made African smallholder agricultural transformation a core, long-standing programme area, funding both direct agricultural research and the institutions that carry it out, including AGRA, which it co-founded with the Rockefeller Foundation. In Sierra Leone specifically, the foundation-backed Lives and Livelihoods Fund, run jointly with the Islamic Development Bank, has supported regional rice value chain projects increasing productivity and market linkages for smallholder farmers, and the foundation separately co-funds IITA's cassava-focused BASICS project and the AfDB-partnered Technologies for African Agricultural Transformation initiative. The foundation's heavy influence over African agricultural research funding has also drawn substantial, well-documented criticism from researchers and farmer organizations concerned about a single private donor shaping public agricultural policy priorities across an entire continent, a debate worth knowing about given how much of this portal's own cited research traces back to foundation-funded institutions.

☪️
Multilateral Development Bank

Islamic Development Bank (IsDB)

A multilateral development bank serving 57 member countries across the Islamic world, IsDB finances development projects using Islamic finance principles, profit-and-loss-sharing structures rather than conventional interest-bearing loans, an important structural distinction from how the World Bank or AfDB typically lend. IsDB co-runs the Lives and Livelihoods Fund together with the Gates Foundation and Middle Eastern donor partners, which has specifically named Sierra Leone among the beneficiary countries for regional rice value chain investment alongside Senegal, Niger, Guinea, and the Gambia. IsDB also co-financed, together with BADEA, the Tormabum Rice Industrial Complex in Bonthe District referenced elsewhere on this portal, directly tying the bank to one of Sierra Leone's flagship rice infrastructure investments.

🛢️
Multilateral Development Fund

OPEC Fund for International Development

Established by OPEC member states in 1976 to provide development financing to other developing countries, separate from but sometimes co-financing alongside larger institutions like IFAD, the OPEC Fund channels oil-exporting nations' capital into infrastructure, agriculture, and finance projects across the developing world. In Sierra Leone, the OPEC Fund has partnered directly with both the national government and with IFAD on an Agricultural Value Chain Development Project aimed at strengthening smallholder market access and value-chain infrastructure. The OPEC Fund's relatively smaller scale compared to bodies like the World Bank often makes it a useful co-financing partner that joins a larger institution's project rather than leading equivalently large standalone programmes on its own.

🇬🇧
Bilateral Agency (UK)

UK FCDO (formerly DFID)

The United Kingdom's Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office, formed in 2020 from a merger that absorbed the former Department for International Development, continues much of DFID's earlier bilateral development financing role in West Africa under its new institutional name. In Sierra Leone, a DFID co-financing grant agreement directly funded capacity-building support for the Sierra Leone Chamber for Agribusiness Development, helping establish its agribusiness forum, train its staff, and refurbish its offices, exactly the kind of institutional groundwork that let SLeCAD later co-implement programmes with USAID, AfDB, and the Gates Foundation. Because of the 2020 merger, older Sierra Leone agricultural programme documents will reference DFID by name even though the same UK funding relationship now runs through FCDO.

🔬
Regional Research Council

CORAF / WECARD

The West and Central African Council for Agricultural Research and Development, commonly known by its French acronym CORAF or the older English acronym WECARD, coordinates agricultural research collaboration and technology-sharing specifically across West and Central African countries, complementing global bodies like CGIAR with a regionally focused mandate. CORAF provided technical support to Sierra Leone's participation in the West Africa Agricultural Transformation Programme, helping strengthen Sierra Leone's National Centre of Specialization for mangrove rice into a Regional Centre of Excellence whose findings are then shared across other CORAF member countries facing similar mangrove rice conditions. This two-way technology-sharing model, Sierra Leone both receiving regional research and contributing its own specialized mangrove rice expertise outward, is a distinctive feature of how CORAF operates compared with donor-recipient relationships that flow in only one direction.

🇸🇱 Sierra Leone National Agricultural Organizations

The domestic institutions, federations, and research centres doing this work day to day, inside Sierra Leone.

🧑‍🌾
National Farmer Federation

NaFFSL — National Federation of Farmers of Sierra Leone

The country's umbrella farmer advocacy body, NaFFSL exists to unite farmers nationwide, conventional and organic alike, and represent their collective interests as Sierra Leone's agricultural sector shifts from subsistence farming toward a more commercial, business-oriented model the federation calls 'Farm for Business'. NaFFSL has received direct capacity-building support from the World Bank and a DFID co-financing grant, funding district-level farmer mobilization structures, staff training, and office infrastructure, and the federation is also a named implementing partner in Sierra Leone's national seed-system investment roadmap alongside SLARI. For an individual smallholder, NaFFSL's district-level structures are typically the first point of contact for joining a recognized farmer organization, which in turn is often a prerequisite for accessing many donor-funded support programmes that work through registered farmer groups rather than reaching individual farmers directly.

🏢
Agribusiness Chamber

SLeCAD — Sierra Leone Chamber for Agribusiness Development

SLeCAD is Sierra Leone's dedicated chamber for agribusiness, established specifically to support the establishment and operation of an agribusiness forum, industrial and value-chain development, and private-sector capacity within the agricultural sector. The chamber has received institutional capacity-building support, staffing, training, and office infrastructure, through a DFID co-financing grant and World Bank-funded programmes, and is named as a key implementing partner alongside major donors including the Gates Foundation and AfDB in Sierra Leone's national agricultural development roadmap. For an agribusiness entrepreneur, SLeCAD functions as the natural first point of contact for navigating value-chain development support, business registration guidance, and connections to the donor-funded programmes increasingly channelled through chambers like this one rather than directly to individual businesses.

👩‍🌾
Women Farmers Network

SLeWOFF — Sierra Leone Women Farmers Forum

SLeWOFF represents the specific interests of Sierra Leone's women farmers, a critical advocacy role given that national agricultural census data identifies more female farmers than male farmers nationally, while women farmers are disproportionately concentrated in unpaid family labour roles rather than paid or self-employed positions, and predominantly grow and process the cassava, groundnut, and vegetable crops profiled extensively on this portal. SLeWOFF is consulted directly in major agricultural project planning, including World Bank-funded programme environmental and social impact assessments, specifically to ensure women farmers' concerns and empowerment needs are reflected before large agricultural investments proceed. For the women's cooperative and gari-processing microenterprises described throughout this portal's crop guides, SLeWOFF is a natural organizing and advocacy structure for negotiating better market access and equipment support.

🌾
Rice Research Station

Rokupr Agricultural Research Centre (RARC)

One of SLARI's specialized research centres, Rokupr has historically been Sierra Leone's lead institution for rice research, with a particular and internationally recognized specialization in mangrove and swamp rice varieties suited to the tidal and flooded growing conditions found along Sierra Leone's coastal river systems. Rokupr's mangrove rice research has been formally elevated into a Regional Centre of Excellence under the West Africa Agricultural Transformation Programme, meaning its findings are now shared across other West African countries facing similar mangrove rice growing conditions rather than staying purely a domestic resource. For a lowland or mangrove rice farmer specifically, Rokupr-developed varieties and growing recommendations are the most locally validated rice guidance available, tested directly under Sierra Leone's own coastal growing conditions rather than adapted from research done elsewhere.

🌦️
Meteorological Agency

Sierra Leone Meteorological Agency (SLMA)

SLMA is the national government body responsible for weather forecasting, climate monitoring, and early warning systems across Sierra Leone, the domestic counterpart and data partner to the kind of hyperlocal AI weather forecasting that companies like Ignitia, profiled elsewhere on this portal, layer additional tropical-specific modelling on top of. SLMA's forecasts and climate records feed into broader national planning, including agricultural planting calendar guidance and flood and drought early warning, and the agency works alongside the National Water Resources Management Agency on water-related agricultural planning concerns, particularly relevant for lowland and mangrove rice growers managing flood depth. For a farmer without access to a subscription weather service, SLMA's publicly broadcast forecasts remain the primary free, locally-grounded source of rainfall and weather outlook information available nationwide.

Organization descriptions reflect general published mandates as of this portal's last update. Programmes, leadership, and funding levels change — confirm current details with each organization's official channels when online.

Closing the Gap

30 Solutions for Sierra Leone to Feed Itself — and Who Has to Act

Food self-sufficiency is not one fix, it is many coordinated ones. Each solution below names the actor with the most direct power to make it happen, grouped by who that actor is.

Government & Ministry of Agriculture
Government

Sustain Feed Salone funding beyond election cycles

Ring-fence agricultural budget lines so flagship programmes survive changes in administration rather than restarting from zero every few years.

Government

Reform and document land tenure

Give farmers and tenants secure, legally recognised rights so they are willing to invest in land improvement, mechanization, and perennial crops.

Government

Expand rural feeder roads strategically

Prioritise road and bridge investment on the specific corridors serving the country's main rice, vegetable, and livestock-producing areas.

Government

Build a national strategic grain reserve

Buy and store surplus harvest to stabilise prices for farmers and protect consumers during lean seasons or import disruptions.

Government

Negotiate a dedicated forex window for agricultural imports

Reduce the cost volatility of imported machinery, fertiliser, fuel, and veterinary supplies that currently undermines farm-level budgeting.

Njala University & Research Institutions
Research

Scale up the National Comprehensive Soil Survey

Complete and publish detailed soil data nationwide so fertiliser and crop-choice advice can be tailored to actual field conditions, not guesswork.

Research

Breed and release more climate-resilient varieties

Continue developing drought- and flood-tolerant rice, cassava, and legume varieties suited to Sierra Leone's shifting rainfall patterns.

Research

Build commercialisation pathways for campus innovations

Create structured handoff mechanisms so machinery and seed prototypes developed on research stations actually reach manufacturers and farmers.

Research

Expand the National Agricultural Training Centre's operator/mechanic pipeline

Certify more rural youth as machinery operators and technicians to close the skills gap repeatedly identified in national audits.

Research

Run applied research directly with farmer cooperatives

Test new techniques on real cooperative-managed plots rather than research-station plots alone, so results transfer faster to working farms.

Banks, Microfinance & Development Partners
Finance

Expand rural credit specifically for smallholders

Grow programmes like the EU/UNCDF-backed Salone Access to Finance project so financing reaches farmers who currently cannot access formal credit at all.

Finance

Design harvest-aligned loan repayment schedules

Match instalment timing to actual harvest and sale dates instead of a fixed monthly calendar that ignores farm cash-flow patterns.

Finance

Introduce affordable crop and livestock insurance

Offer products smallholders can realistically afford, so one bad season, disease outbreak, or flood does not permanently end a farming livelihood.

Finance

Blend donor and government financing for infrastructure

Combine instruments like the $75 million Special Agro-Processing Zone project with World Bank and IFAD rural finance support to close the equipment-credit gap.

Finance

Fund decentralised spare-parts depots outside Freetown

Finance district-level repair workshops and parts stock so breakdowns are fixed in days, not the weeks currently common in remote areas.

Private Sector & Agribusiness
Private Sector

Invest in local milling, processing, and cold storage

Capture more value inside Sierra Leone instead of exporting raw, unprocessed produce and importing back the processed equivalent.

Private Sector

Expand contract farming and off-taker agreements

Guarantee farmers a buyer and a fair price in exchange for a reliable supply, aligning processor and farmer interests directly.

Private Sector

Build feed-milling capacity for poultry, pig, and fish farmers

Supply affordable, good-quality local feed to remove one of the biggest cost barriers facing livestock and aquaculture enterprises.

Private Sector

Commercialise locally adapted machinery

Manufacture or assemble appropriately sized tools, such as small power tillers, suited to Sierra Leone's typical farm sizes and terrain.

Private Sector

Run mobile mechanization hire services on a published schedule

Move equipment systematically between communities so farmers can plan planting around guaranteed, well-publicised service availability.

Farmers & Cooperatives
Farmers

Form or join marketing and equipment-sharing cooperatives

Pool volume for better prices and share machinery cost and risk rather than each farmer trying to own equipment individually.

Farmers

Adopt basic record keeping

Track feed/input costs, losses, and actual sale prices so real profitability, not assumption, drives planting and enterprise decisions.

Farmers

Diversify into under-exploited enterprises

Add beekeeping, aquaculture, mushroom cultivation, or grasscutter farming alongside conventional crops or livestock to spread risk and income sources.

Farmers

Practise crop rotation and integrated pest management

Protect soil fertility and cut pesticide costs by rotating crops and scouting for pests instead of spraying on a fixed calendar.

Farmers

Organise into voluntary block-farming arrangements

Combine fragmented neighbouring plots for shared mechanized cultivation while each farmer retains individual ownership of their land.

Communities, Schools & Civil Society
Community

Run farmer-to-farmer demonstration plots

Let a respected local lead farmer show new techniques on their own field — peer demonstration shifts opinion faster than outside promotion.

Community

Design training sessions around women's schedules and mobility

Schedule timing, location, and childcare support so women, who do a large share of farm labour, can actually attend and benefit.

Community

Build secure community storage and machinery sheds

Protect shared equipment and harvested produce from theft, pests, and weather damage under clear, jointly agreed maintenance custodianship.

Community

Integrate basic literacy and numeracy into farmer training

Help farmers read manuals, follow safety procedures, and manage simple costing, removing literacy as a barrier to adopting new methods.

Community

Make farming visible and attractive to youth

Showcase real career pathways — operator, mechanic, agronomist, equipment-hire entrepreneur — so young people see farming as a viable, modern livelihood rather than a fallback.

No single actor on this list can deliver food self-sufficiency alone. The pattern across Sierra Leone's own audits and donor evaluations is consistent: progress stalls whenever hardware, finance, training, and policy are not advanced together by the actors best placed to lead each piece.

211 Questions, Answered Offline

Frequently Asked Questions

Search any question, or filter by who you are, farmer, student, teacher, operator, mechanic, youth, or institution, plus dedicated sections on crops, calculators, and AI tools.

🔎
About & Share

Advertise This Department's Work

Ready-to-use copy for sharing this portal and the Department's mechanization expertise online, plus the offline distribution method for everyone without internet access.

📘 About the Department

The Department of Agricultural Engineering, School of Technology, Njala University, is Sierra Leone's lead academic authority on agricultural mechanization — training the operators, mechanics, engineers, and extension officers who are turning Feed Salone's national ambitions into working tillage, threshing, and processing capacity on the ground. This portal is one expression of that mandate: a free, offline, single-file knowledge base built so that mechanization expertise reaches a farmer in a no-signal village exactly as completely as it reaches a student in a Freetown lecture hall.

📘 Facebook / LinkedIn Caption — Launch Post

📘 Facebook / LinkedIn Caption — Distribution Call-Out

  • Post the link once, transfer it forever: Share this page's download link one time on the Department's Facebook or LinkedIn page using either caption above.
  • Direct people to Xender / Bluetooth: Instruct readers to open the file in any browser, then use Xender, SHAREit, or Bluetooth to pass the single HTML file to neighbors, students, and co-workers who have no internet access.
  • Tag local extension offices: Tag district agricultural offices and teacher-training colleges in the post so they can redistribute the file physically at their next field visit or training session.
Sierra Leone Agricultural Knowledge Portal · Department of Agricultural Engineering, School of Technology, Njala University · Principal Investigator & Architect: Sahr Gevao, Lead Educator & Agriculturist · Runs 100% offline after first download.
